1	-------------------------------- MODULE Sets --------------------------------
2	EXTENDS Integers, NaturalsInduction, TLAPS
3	
4	IsBijection(f, S, T) == /\ f \in [S -> T]
5	                        /\ \A x, y \in S : (x # y) => (f[x] # f[y])
6	                        /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : f[x] = y
7	
8	IsFiniteSet(S) == \E n \in Nat : \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
9	
10	CONSTANT Cardinality(_)
11	AXIOM CardinalityAxiom ==
12	         \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) =>
13	           \A n : (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
14	                    (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
15	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
16	
17	THEOREM CardinalityInNat == \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => Cardinality(S) \in Nat
18	  PROOF OMITTED
19	
20	------------------------------------------------------------------
21	
22	THEOREM CardinalityZero ==
23	           /\ IsFiniteSet({})
24	           /\ Cardinality({}) = 0
25	           /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ (Cardinality(S)=0) => (S = {})
26	  PROOF OMITTED
27	
28	THEOREM CardinalityPlusOne ==
29	    ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
30	           NEW x, x \notin S
31	    PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup {x})
32	           /\ Cardinality(S \cup {x}) = Cardinality(S) + 1
33	  PROOF OMITTED
34	
35	------------------------------------------------------------------
36	
37	THEOREM CardinalityOne == \A m : /\ IsFiniteSet({m})
38	                                 /\ Cardinality({m}) = 1
39	  PROOF OMITTED
40	
41	THEOREM CardinalityTwo == \A m, p : m # p => 
42	                              /\ IsFiniteSet({m,p})
43	                              /\ Cardinality({m,p}) = 2
44	  PROOF OMITTED
45	
46	THEOREM IntervalCardinality ==  
47	  ASSUME NEW a \in Nat, NEW b \in Nat 
48	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(a..b)
49	         /\ Cardinality(a..b) = IF a > b THEN 0 ELSE b-a+1
50	  PROOF OMITTED
51	
52	------------------------------------------------------------------
53	
54	THEOREM CardinalityOneConverse ==
55	   ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), Cardinality(S) = 1
56	   PROVE  \E m : S = {m}
57	  PROOF OMITTED
58	
59	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
60	
61	THEOREM IsBijectionInverse ==
62	  ASSUME NEW f, NEW S, NEW T, 
63	         IsBijection(f, S, T) 
64	  PROVE  \E g : IsBijection(g, T, S)
65	  PROOF OMITTED
66	
67	THEOREM IsBijectionTransitive ==
68	  ASSUME NEW f1, NEW f2, NEW S, NEW T, NEW U, 
69	           IsBijection(f1, S, U),
70	           IsBijection(f2, U, T) 
71	  PROVE  \E g : IsBijection(g, S, T)
72	  PROOF OMITTED
73	
74	THEOREM IsBijectionCardinality ==
75	  \A f, S, T : /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
76	               /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
77	               => (IsBijection(f, S, T) <=> Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T))
78	  PROOF OMITTED
79	
80	LEMMA CardinalitySetMinus ==
81	      ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
82	             NEW x \in S
83	      PROVE /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ {x})
84	            /\ Cardinality(S \ {x}) = Cardinality(S) - 1
85	  PROOF OMITTED
86	
87	THEOREM FiniteSubset ==
88	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW TT, IsFiniteSet(TT), S \subseteq TT
89	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
90	         /\ Cardinality(S) \leq Cardinality(TT)
91	  PROOF OMITTED
92	
93	-------------------------------------------------------

95	THEOREM CardinalityUnion ==
96	          \A S, T : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ IsFiniteSet(T) =>
97	                      /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup T)
98	                      /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cap T)
99	                      /\ Cardinality(S \cup T) =
100	                              Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T)
101	                              - Cardinality(S \cap T)  
102	  PROOF OMITTED
103	
104	-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
105	
106	THEOREM PigeonHole ==
107	            \A S, T : /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
108	                      /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
109	                      /\ Cardinality(T) < Cardinality(S)
110	                      => \A f \in [S -> T] :
111	                           \E x, y \in S : x # y /\ f[x] = f[y]
112	  PROOF OMITTED

115	
116	THEOREM \A S, T , f :  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
117	                       /\ f \in [S -> T]
118	                       /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : y = f[x]
119	                       => /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
120	                          /\ Cardinality(T) \leq Cardinality(S)
121	PROOF OMITTED
122	
123	THEOREM ProductFinite ==
124	     \A S, T : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ IsFiniteSet(T) => IsFiniteSet(S \X T)
125	PROOF OMITTED
126

-------------------------------- MODULE Sets --------------------------------
EXTENDS Integers, NaturalsInduction, TLAPS

IsBijection(f, S, T) == /\ f \in [S -> T]
                        /\ \A x, y \in S : (x # y) => (f[x] # f[y])
                        /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : f[x] = y

IsFiniteSet(S) == \E n \in Nat : \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)

CONSTANT Cardinality(_)
AXIOM CardinalityAxiom ==
         \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) =>
           \A n : (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
                    (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM CardinalityInNat == \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => Cardinality(S) \in Nat
  PROOF OMITTED

------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM CardinalityZero ==
           /\ IsFiniteSet({})
           /\ Cardinality({}) = 0
           /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ (Cardinality(S)=0) => (S = {})
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM CardinalityPlusOne ==
    ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
           NEW x, x \notin S
    PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup {x})
           /\ Cardinality(S \cup {x}) = Cardinality(S) + 1
  PROOF OMITTED

------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM CardinalityOne == \A m : /\ IsFiniteSet({m})
                                 /\ Cardinality({m}) = 1
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM CardinalityTwo == \A m, p : m # p => 
                              /\ IsFiniteSet({m,p})
                              /\ Cardinality({m,p}) = 2
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM IntervalCardinality ==  
  ASSUME NEW a \in Nat, NEW b \in Nat 
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(a..b)
         /\ Cardinality(a..b) = IF a > b THEN 0 ELSE b-a+1
  PROOF OMITTED

------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM CardinalityOneConverse ==
   ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), Cardinality(S) = 1
   PROVE  \E m : S = {m}
  PROOF OMITTED

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM IsBijectionInverse ==
  ASSUME NEW f, NEW S, NEW T, 
         IsBijection(f, S, T) 
  PROVE  \E g : IsBijection(g, T, S)
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM IsBijectionTransitive ==
  ASSUME NEW f1, NEW f2, NEW S, NEW T, NEW U, 
           IsBijection(f1, S, U),
           IsBijection(f2, U, T) 
  PROVE  \E g : IsBijection(g, S, T)
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM IsBijectionCardinality ==
  \A f, S, T : /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
               /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
               => (IsBijection(f, S, T) <=> Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T))
  PROOF OMITTED

LEMMA CardinalitySetMinus ==
      ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
             NEW x \in S
      PROVE /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ {x})
            /\ Cardinality(S \ {x}) = Cardinality(S) - 1
  PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM FiniteSubset ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW TT, IsFiniteSet(TT), S \subseteq TT
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
         /\ Cardinality(S) \leq Cardinality(TT)
  PROOF OMITTED

-------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM CardinalityUnion ==
          \A S, T : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ IsFiniteSet(T) =>
                      /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup T)
                      /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cap T)
                      /\ Cardinality(S \cup T) =
                              Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T)
                              - Cardinality(S \cap T)  
  PROOF OMITTED

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM PigeonHole ==
            \A S, T : /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
                      /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
                      /\ Cardinality(T) < Cardinality(S)
                      => \A f \in [S -> T] :
                           \E x, y \in S : x # y /\ f[x] = f[y]
  PROOF OMITTED

-------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM \A S, T , f :  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
                       /\ f \in [S -> T]
                       /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : y = f[x]
                       => /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
                          /\ Cardinality(T) \leq Cardinality(S)
PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM ProductFinite ==
     \A S, T : IsFiniteSet(S) /\ IsFiniteSet(T) => IsFiniteSet(S \X T)
PROOF OMITTED

THEOREM SubsetsFinite == \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => IsFiniteSet(SUBSET S)
PROOF OMITTED
=============================================================================

1	------------------------- MODULE NaturalsInduction --------------------------
2	(***************************************************************************)
3	(* This module contains useful theorems for inductive proofs and recursive *)
4	(* definitions over the naturals.                                          *)
5	(*                                                                         *)
6	(* Some of the statements of the theorems are decomposed in terms of       *)
7	(* definitions.  This is done for two reasons:                             *)
8	(*                                                                         *)
9	(*  - It makes it easier for the backends to instantiate the theorems      *)
10	(*    when those definitions are not expanded.                             *)
11	(*                                                                         *)
12	(*  - It can be convenient when writing proofs to use those definitions    *)
13	(*    rather than having to write out their expansions.                    *)
14	(***************************************************************************)
15	EXTENDS Integers
16	
17	(***************************************************************************)
18	(* The following is the simple statement of inductions over the naturals.  *)
19	(* For predicates P defined by a moderately complex operator, it is often  *)
20	(* useful to hide the operator definition before using this theorem. That  *)
21	(* is, you first define a suitable operator P (not necessarily by that     *)
22	(* name), prove the two hypotheses of the theorem, and then hide the       *)
23	(* definition of P when using the theorem.                                 *)
24	(***************************************************************************)
25	THEOREM NatInduction ==
26	  ASSUME NEW P(_),
27	         P(0),
28	         \A n \in Nat : P(n) => P(n+1)
29	  PROVE  \A n \in Nat : P(n)
30	
31	(***************************************************************************)
32	(* A useful corollary of NatInduction                                      *)
33	(***************************************************************************)
34	THEOREM DownwardNatInduction ==
35	  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW m \in Nat, P(m),
36	         \A n \in 1 .. m : P(n) => P(n-1)
37	  PROVE  P(0)
38	
39	(***************************************************************************)
40	(* The following theorem expresses a stronger induction principle,         *)
41	(* also known as course-of-values induction, where the induction           *)
42	(* hypothesis is available for all strictly smaller natural numbers.       *)
43	(***************************************************************************)
44	THEOREM GeneralNatInduction ==
45	          ASSUME NEW P(_),
46	                 \A n \in Nat : (\A m \in 0..(n-1) : P(m)) => P(n)
47	          PROVE  \A n \in Nat : P(n)
48	
49	(***************************************************************************)
50	(* The following theorem expresses the ``least-number principle'':         *)
51	(* if P(n) is true for some natural number n then there is a               *)
52	(* smallest natural number for which P is true. It could be derived in     *)
53	(* module WellFoundedInduction as a corollary of the fact that the natural *)
54	(* numbers are well ordered, but we give a direct proof.                   *)
55	(***************************************************************************)
56	THEOREM SmallestNatural ==
57	  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW n \in Nat, P(n)
58	  PROVE  \E m \in Nat : /\ P(m)
59	                        /\ \A k \in 0 .. m-1 : ~ P(k)
60	
61	(***************************************************************************)
62	(* The following theorem says that a recursively defined function f over   *)
63	(* the natural numbers is well-defined if for every n \in Nat the          *)
64	(* definition of f[n] depends only on arguments smaller than n.            *)
65	(***************************************************************************)
66	THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNat ==
67	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_),
68	         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW g, NEW h,
69	                \A i \in 0..(n-1) : g[i] = h[i]
70	         PROVE  Def(g, n) = Def(h, n)
71	  PROVE  LET f[n \in Nat] == Def(f, n)
72	         IN  f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f, n)]
73	
74	(***************************************************************************)
75	(* The following theorem NatInductiveDef is what you use to justify a      *)
76	(* function defined by primitive recursion over the naturals.              *)
77	(***************************************************************************)
78	NatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, f0, Def(_,_)) ==
79	   (f =  CHOOSE g : g = [i \in Nat |-> IF i = 0 THEN f0 ELSE Def(g[i-1], i)])
80	NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def(_,_)) ==
81	     f = [i \in Nat |-> IF i = 0 THEN f0 ELSE Def(f[i-1], i)]
82	
83	THEOREM NatInductiveDef ==
84	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW f0,
85	         NatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, f0, Def)
86	  PROVE  NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def)
87	
88	(***************************************************************************)
89	(* The following two theorems allow you to prove the type of a recursively *)
90	(* defined function over the natural numbers.                              *)
91	(***************************************************************************)
92	THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNatType ==
93	  ASSUME NEW f, NEW S, NEW Def(_,_), f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f,n)],
94	         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW g, \A i \in 0 .. n-1 : g[i] \in S
95	         PROVE  Def(g,n) \in S
96	  PROVE  f \in [Nat -> S]
97	
98	THEOREM NatInductiveDefType ==
99	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW S, NEW f, NEW f0 \in S,
100	         NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def),
101	         f0 \in S,
102	         \A v \in S, n \in Nat \ {0} : Def(v, n) \in S
103	  PROVE  f \in [Nat -> S]
104	
105	(***************************************************************************)
106	(* The following theorems show uniqueness of functions recursively defined *)
107	(* over Nat.                                                               *)
108	(***************************************************************************)
109	THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNatUnique ==
110	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g,
111	         f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f,n)],
112	         g = [n \in Nat |-> Def(g,n)],
113	         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW ff, NEW gg,
114	                \A i \in 0..(n-1) : ff[i] = gg[i]
115	         PROVE  Def(ff, n) = Def(gg, n)
116	  PROVE  f = g
117	
118	THEOREM NatInductiveUnique ==
119	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g, NEW f0,
120	         NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def),
121	         NatInductiveDefConclusion(g, f0, Def)
122	  PROVE  f = g
123	
124	(***************************************************************************)
125	(* The following theorems are analogous to the preceding ones but for      *)
126	(* functions defined over intervals of natural numbers.                    *)
127	(***************************************************************************)
128	
129	FiniteNatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, c, Def(_,_), m, n) ==
130	   (f =  CHOOSE g : g = [i \in m..n |-> IF i = m THEN c ELSE Def(g[i-1], i)])
131	FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def(_,_), m, n) ==
132	     f = [i \in m..n |-> IF i = m THEN c ELSE Def(f[i-1], i)]
133	
134	THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveDef ==
135	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW c, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
136	         FiniteNatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, c, Def, m, n)
137	  PROVE  FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n)
138	
139	THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveDefType ==
140	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW c \in S, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
141	         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n),
142	         \A v \in S, i \in (m+1) .. n : Def(v,i) \in S
143	  PROVE  f \in [m..n -> S]
144	
145	THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveUnique ==
146	  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g, NEW c, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
147	         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n),
148	         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(g, c, Def, m, n)
149	  PROVE  f = g
150	
151	=============================================================================
152	
153	(***************************************************************************)
154	(* The following example shows how this module is used.                    *)
155	(***************************************************************************)
156	
157	factorial[n \in Nat] == IF n = 0 THEN 1 ELSE n * factorial[n-1]
158	
159	THEOREM FactorialDefConclusion == NatInductiveDefConclusion(factorial, 1, LAMBDA v,n : n*v)
160	<1>1. NatInductiveDefHypothesis(factorial, 1, LAMBDA v,n : n*v)
161	  BY DEF NatInductiveDefHypothesis, factorial
162	<1>2. QED
163	  BY <1>1, NatInductiveDef
164	
165	THEOREM FactorialDef == \A n \in Nat : factorial[n] = IF n = 0 THEN 1 ELSE n * factorial[n-1]
166	BY FactorialDefConclusion DEF NatInductiveDefConclusion
167	
168	THEOREM FactorialType == factorial \in [Nat -> Nat]
169	<1>1. \A v \in Nat, n \in Nat \ {0} : n * v \in Nat
170	  OBVIOUS
171	<1>2. QED
172	  BY <1>1, NatInductiveDefType, FactorialDefConclusion, Isa
173	
174

176	\* Last modified Thu Aug 22 15:29:20 CEST 2019 by merz
177	\* Last modified Tue Oct 15 12:06:48 CEST 2013 by shaolin
178	\* Last modified Sat Nov 26 08:49:59 CET 2011 by merz

------------------------- MODULE NaturalsInduction --------------------------
(***************************************************************************)
(* This module contains useful theorems for inductive proofs and recursive *)
(* definitions over the naturals.                                          *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Some of the statements of the theorems are decomposed in terms of       *)
(* definitions.  This is done for two reasons:                             *)
(*                                                                         *)
(*  - It makes it easier for the backends to instantiate the theorems      *)
(*    when those definitions are not expanded.                             *)
(*                                                                         *)
(*  - It can be convenient when writing proofs to use those definitions    *)
(*    rather than having to write out their expansions.                    *)
(***************************************************************************)
EXTENDS Integers

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following is the simple statement of inductions over the naturals.  *)
(* For predicates P defined by a moderately complex operator, it is often  *)
(* useful to hide the operator definition before using this theorem. That  *)
(* is, you first define a suitable operator P (not necessarily by that     *)
(* name), prove the two hypotheses of the theorem, and then hide the       *)
(* definition of P when using the theorem.                                 *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM NatInduction ==
  ASSUME NEW P(_),
         P(0),
         \A n \in Nat : P(n) => P(n+1)
  PROVE  \A n \in Nat : P(n)

(***************************************************************************)
(* A useful corollary of NatInduction                                      *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM DownwardNatInduction ==
  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW m \in Nat, P(m),
         \A n \in 1 .. m : P(n) => P(n-1)
  PROVE  P(0)

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem expresses a stronger induction principle,         *)
(* also known as course-of-values induction, where the induction           *)
(* hypothesis is available for all strictly smaller natural numbers.       *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM GeneralNatInduction ==
          ASSUME NEW P(_),
                 \A n \in Nat : (\A m \in 0..(n-1) : P(m)) => P(n)
          PROVE  \A n \in Nat : P(n)

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem expresses the ``least-number principle'':         *)
(* if P(n) is true for some natural number n then there is a               *)
(* smallest natural number for which P is true. It could be derived in     *)
(* module WellFoundedInduction as a corollary of the fact that the natural *)
(* numbers are well ordered, but we give a direct proof.                   *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM SmallestNatural ==
  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW n \in Nat, P(n)
  PROVE  \E m \in Nat : /\ P(m)
                        /\ \A k \in 0 .. m-1 : ~ P(k)

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem says that a recursively defined function f over   *)
(* the natural numbers is well-defined if for every n \in Nat the          *)
(* definition of f[n] depends only on arguments smaller than n.            *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNat ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_),
         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW g, NEW h,
                \A i \in 0..(n-1) : g[i] = h[i]
         PROVE  Def(g, n) = Def(h, n)
  PROVE  LET f[n \in Nat] == Def(f, n)
         IN  f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f, n)]

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorem NatInductiveDef is what you use to justify a      *)
(* function defined by primitive recursion over the naturals.              *)
(***************************************************************************)
NatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, f0, Def(_,_)) ==
   (f =  CHOOSE g : g = [i \in Nat |-> IF i = 0 THEN f0 ELSE Def(g[i-1], i)])
NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def(_,_)) ==
     f = [i \in Nat |-> IF i = 0 THEN f0 ELSE Def(f[i-1], i)]

THEOREM NatInductiveDef ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW f0,
         NatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, f0, Def)
  PROVE  NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def)

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following two theorems allow you to prove the type of a recursively *)
(* defined function over the natural numbers.                              *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNatType ==
  ASSUME NEW f, NEW S, NEW Def(_,_), f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f,n)],
         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW g, \A i \in 0 .. n-1 : g[i] \in S
         PROVE  Def(g,n) \in S
  PROVE  f \in [Nat -> S]

THEOREM NatInductiveDefType ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW S, NEW f, NEW f0 \in S,
         NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def),
         f0 \in S,
         \A v \in S, n \in Nat \ {0} : Def(v, n) \in S
  PROVE  f \in [Nat -> S]

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorems show uniqueness of functions recursively defined *)
(* over Nat.                                                               *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM RecursiveFcnOfNatUnique ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g,
         f = [n \in Nat |-> Def(f,n)],
         g = [n \in Nat |-> Def(g,n)],
         ASSUME NEW n \in Nat, NEW ff, NEW gg,
                \A i \in 0..(n-1) : ff[i] = gg[i]
         PROVE  Def(ff, n) = Def(gg, n)
  PROVE  f = g

THEOREM NatInductiveUnique ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g, NEW f0,
         NatInductiveDefConclusion(f, f0, Def),
         NatInductiveDefConclusion(g, f0, Def)
  PROVE  f = g

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following theorems are analogous to the preceding ones but for      *)
(* functions defined over intervals of natural numbers.                    *)
(***************************************************************************)

FiniteNatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, c, Def(_,_), m, n) ==
   (f =  CHOOSE g : g = [i \in m..n |-> IF i = m THEN c ELSE Def(g[i-1], i)])
FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def(_,_), m, n) ==
     f = [i \in m..n |-> IF i = m THEN c ELSE Def(f[i-1], i)]

THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveDef ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW c, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
         FiniteNatInductiveDefHypothesis(f, c, Def, m, n)
  PROVE  FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n)

THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveDefType ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW c \in S, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n),
         \A v \in S, i \in (m+1) .. n : Def(v,i) \in S
  PROVE  f \in [m..n -> S]

THEOREM FiniteNatInductiveUnique ==
  ASSUME NEW Def(_,_), NEW f, NEW g, NEW c, NEW m \in Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(f, c, Def, m, n),
         FiniteNatInductiveDefConclusion(g, c, Def, m, n)
  PROVE  f = g

=============================================================================

(***************************************************************************)
(* The following example shows how this module is used.                    *)
(***************************************************************************)

factorial[n \in Nat] == IF n = 0 THEN 1 ELSE n * factorial[n-1]

THEOREM FactorialDefConclusion == NatInductiveDefConclusion(factorial, 1, LAMBDA v,n : n*v)
<1>1. NatInductiveDefHypothesis(factorial, 1, LAMBDA v,n : n*v)
  BY DEF NatInductiveDefHypothesis, factorial
<1>2. QED
  BY <1>1, NatInductiveDef

THEOREM FactorialDef == \A n \in Nat : factorial[n] = IF n = 0 THEN 1 ELSE n * factorial[n-1]
BY FactorialDefConclusion DEF NatInductiveDefConclusion

THEOREM FactorialType == factorial \in [Nat -> Nat]
<1>1. \A v \in Nat, n \in Nat \ {0} : n * v \in Nat
  OBVIOUS
<1>2. QED
  BY <1>1, NatInductiveDefType, FactorialDefConclusion, Isa

1	------------------------- MODULE FiniteSetTheorems --------------------------
2	(***************************************************************************)
3	(* `^{\large\bf \vspace{12pt}                                              *)
4	(*  Facts about finite sets and their cardinality.                         *)
5	(*  Originally contributed by Tom Rodeheffer, MSR.                         *)
6	(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
7	(***************************************************************************)
8	
9	EXTENDS FiniteSets, Integers, Functions, WellFoundedInduction
10	
11	(***************************************************************************)
12	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
13	(*                                                                         *)
14	(* A set S is finite iff there exists a natural number n such that there   *)
15	(* exist a surjection (or a bijection) from 1..n to S.                     *)
16	(*                                                                         *)
17	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
18	(***************************************************************************)
19	
20	LEMMA FS_NatSurjection ==
21	  ASSUME NEW S
22	  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(S) <=> \E n \in Nat : ExistsSurjection(1..n,S)
23	
24	LEMMA FS_NatBijection ==
25	  ASSUME NEW S
26	  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(S) <=> \E n \in Nat : ExistsBijection(1..n,S)
27	
28	
29	(***************************************************************************)
30	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
31	(*                                                                         *)
32	(* If there exists n \in Nat such that a bijection exists from 1..n to S,  *)
33	(* then Cardinality(S) = n.                                                *)
34	(*                                                                         *)
35	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
36	(***************************************************************************)
37	
38	LEMMA FS_CountingElements ==
39	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..n,S)
40	  PROVE  Cardinality(S) = n
41	
42	
43	(***************************************************************************)
44	(* Corollary: a surjection from 1..n to S provides a cardinality bound.    *)
45	(***************************************************************************)
46	THEOREM FS_SurjCardinalityBound ==
47	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsSurjection(1..n, S)
48	  PROVE  Cardinality(S) <= n
49	
50	
51	(***************************************************************************)
52	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
53	(*                                                                         *)
54	(* For any finite set S, Cardinality(S) \in Nat. Moreover, there is a      *)
55	(* bijection from 1 .. Cardinality(S) to S.                                *)
56	(*                                                                         *)
57	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
58	(***************************************************************************)
59	THEOREM FS_CardinalityType ==
60	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
61	  PROVE  /\ Cardinality(S) \in Nat
62	         /\ ExistsBijection(1..Cardinality(S), S)
63	
64	
65	(***************************************************************************)
66	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
67	(*                                                                         *)
68	(* The image of a finite set under a bijection is finite.                  *)
69	(*                                                                         *)
70	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
71	(***************************************************************************)
72	THEOREM FS_Bijection ==
73	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), ExistsBijection(S,T)
74	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
75	         /\ Cardinality(T) = Cardinality(S)
76	
77	THEOREM FS_SameCardinalityBij ==
78	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), IsFiniteSet(T),
79	         Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T)
80	  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)
81	
82	
83	(***************************************************************************)
84	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
85	(*                                                                         *)
86	(* Any surjection between two finite sets of equal cardinality is          *)
87	(* an injection.                                                           *)
88	(*                                                                         *)
89	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
90	(***************************************************************************)
91	THEOREM FS_SurjSameCardinalityImpliesInj ==
92	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T),
93	         NEW f \in Surjection(S,T)
94	  PROVE  f \in Injection(S,T)
95	
96	
97	(***************************************************************************)
98	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
99	(*                                                                         *)
100	(* The image of a finite set is finite.                                    *)
101	(* NB: Note that any function is a surjection on its range by theorem      *)
102	(*     Fun_RangeProperties.                                                *)
103	(*                                                                         *)
104	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
105	(***************************************************************************)
106	THEOREM FS_Surjection ==
107	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Surjection(S,T), IsFiniteSet(S)
108	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
109	         /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)
110	         /\ Cardinality(T) = Cardinality(S) <=> f \in Injection(S,T)
111	
112	THEOREM FS_Image ==
113	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW Op(_)
114	  PROVE  LET T == {Op(x) : x \in S}
115	         IN  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
116	             /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)
117	
118	(***************************************************************************)
119	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
120	(*                                                                         *)
121	(* Symmetric theorem for injections and the pigeonhole principle.          *)
122	(*                                                                         *)
123	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
124	(***************************************************************************)
125	THEOREM FS_Injection ==
126	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Injection(S,T), IsFiniteSet(T)
127	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
128	         /\ Cardinality(S) <= Cardinality(T)
129	         /\ Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T) <=> f \in Surjection(S,T)
130	
131	THEOREM FS_PigeonHole ==
132	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
133	         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T), Cardinality(T) < Cardinality(S),
134	         NEW f \in [S -> T]
135	  PROVE  \E x,y \in S : x # y /\ f[x] = f[y]
136	
137	
138	(***************************************************************************)
139	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
140	(*                                                                         *)
141	(* The cardinality of a finite set S is 0 iff S is empty.                  *)
142	(*                                                                         *)
143	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
144	(***************************************************************************)
145	THEOREM FS_EmptySet ==
146	  /\ IsFiniteSet({})
147	  /\ Cardinality({}) = 0
148	  /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => (Cardinality(S) = 0 <=> S = {})
149	
150	
151	(***************************************************************************)
152	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
153	(*                                                                         *)
154	(* If S is finite, so are S \cup {x} and S \ {x}.                          *)
155	(*                                                                         *)
156	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
157	(***************************************************************************)
158	THEOREM FS_AddElement ==
159	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW x, IsFiniteSet(S)
160	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup {x})
161	         /\ Cardinality(S \cup {x}) =
162	            IF x \in S THEN Cardinality(S) ELSE Cardinality(S)+1
163	
164	THEOREM FS_RemoveElement ==
165	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW x, IsFiniteSet(S)
166	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ {x})
167	         /\ Cardinality(S \ {x}) =
168	            IF x \in S THEN Cardinality(S)-1 ELSE Cardinality(S)
169	
170	
171	(***************************************************************************)
172	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
173	(*                                                                         *)
174	(* In particular, a singleton set is finite.                               *)
175	(*                                                                         *)
176	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
177	(***************************************************************************)
178	THEOREM FS_Singleton ==
179	  /\ \A x : IsFiniteSet({x}) /\ Cardinality({x}) = 1
180	  /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => (Cardinality(S) = 1 <=> \E x: S = {x})
181	
182	
183	(***************************************************************************)
184	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
185	(*                                                                         *)
186	(* Any subset of a finite set is finite.                                   *)
187	(*                                                                         *)
188	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
189	(***************************************************************************)
190	THEOREM FS_Subset ==
191	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T \in SUBSET S
192	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
193	         /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)
194	         /\ Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T) => S = T
195	
196	
197	(***************************************************************************)
198	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
199	(*                                                                         *)
200	(* a..b is a finite set for any a,b \in Int.                               *)
201	(*                                                                         *)
202	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
203	(***************************************************************************)
204	THEOREM FS_Interval ==
205	  ASSUME NEW a \in Int, NEW b \in Int
206	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(a..b)
207	         /\ Cardinality(a..b) = IF a > b THEN 0 ELSE b-a+1
208	
209	THEOREM FS_BoundedSetOfNaturals ==
210	  ASSUME NEW S \in SUBSET Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
211	         \A s \in S : s <= n
212	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
213	         /\ Cardinality(S) \leq n+1
214	
215	
216	(***************************************************************************)
217	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
218	(*                                                                         *)
219	(* Induction for finite sets.                                              *)
220	(*                                                                         *)
221	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
222	(***************************************************************************)
223	
224	THEOREM FS_Induction ==
225	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
226	         NEW P(_), P({}),
227	         ASSUME NEW T \in SUBSET S, IsFiniteSet(T), P(T), NEW x \in S \ T
228	         PROVE  P(T \cup {x})
229	  PROVE  P(S)
230	
231	
232	(***************************************************************************)
233	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
234	(*                                                                         *)
235	(* The finite subsets form a well-founded ordering with respect to strict  *)
236	(* set inclusion.                                                          *)
237	(*                                                                         *)
238	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
239	(***************************************************************************)
240	
241	FiniteSubsetsOf(S) == { T \in SUBSET S : IsFiniteSet(T) }
242	StrictSubsetOrdering(S) == { ss \in (SUBSET S) \X (SUBSET S) :
243	                                ss[1] \subseteq ss[2] /\ ss[1] # ss[2] }
244	
245	LEMMA FS_FiniteSubsetsOfFinite ==
246	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
247	  PROVE  FiniteSubsetsOf(S) = SUBSET S
248	
249	
250	(*****************************************************************************)
251	(*  The formulation of the following theorem doesn't require S being finite. *)
252	(*  If S is finite, it implies                                               *)
253	(*       IsWellFoundedOn(StrictSubsetOrdering(S), SUBSET S)                  *)
254	(*  using lemma FS_FiniteSubsetsOfFinite.                                    *)
255	(*****************************************************************************)
256	THEOREM FS_StrictSubsetOrderingWellFounded ==
257	  ASSUME NEW S
258	  PROVE  IsWellFoundedOn(StrictSubsetOrdering(S), FiniteSubsetsOf(S))
259	
260	
261	(***************************************************************************)
262	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
263	(*                                                                         *)
264	(* Well-founded induction for finite subsets.                              *)
265	(*                                                                         *)
266	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
267	(***************************************************************************)
268	
269	THEOREM FS_WFInduction ==
270	  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
271	         ASSUME NEW T \in SUBSET S,
272	                \A U \in (SUBSET T) \ {T} : P(U)
273	         PROVE  P(T)
274	  PROVE  P(S)
275	
276	
277	(***************************************************************************)
278	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
279	(*                                                                         *)
280	(* The union of two finite sets is finite.                                 *)
281	(*                                                                         *)
282	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
283	(***************************************************************************)
284	THEOREM FS_Union ==
285	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
286	         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T)
287	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup T)
288	         /\ Cardinality(S \cup T) =
289	               Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T) - Cardinality(S \cap T)
290	
291	
292	(***************************************************************************)
293	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
294	(*                                                                         *)
295	(* Corollary: two majorities intersect. More precisely, any two subsets    *)
296	(* of a finite set U such that the sum of cardinalities of the subsets     *)
297	(* exceeds that of U must have non-empty intersection.                     *)
298	(*                                                                         *)
299	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
300	(***************************************************************************)
301	THEOREM FS_MajoritiesIntersect ==
302	  ASSUME NEW U, NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(U),
303	         S \subseteq U, T \subseteq U,
304	         Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T) > Cardinality(U)
305	  PROVE  S \cap T # {}
306	
307	
308	(***************************************************************************)
309	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
310	(*                                                                         *)
311	(* The intersection of a finite set with an arbitrary set is finite.       *)
312	(*                                                                         *)
313	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
314	(***************************************************************************)
315	
316	
317	THEOREM FS_Intersection ==
318	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T
319	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cap T)
320	         /\ IsFiniteSet(T \cap S)
321	         /\ Cardinality(S \cap T) <= Cardinality(S)
322	         /\ Cardinality(T \cap S) <= Cardinality(S)
323	
324	
325	(***************************************************************************)
326	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
327	(*                                                                         *)
328	(* The difference between a finite set and an arbitrary set is finite.     *)
329	(*                                                                         *)
330	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
331	(***************************************************************************)
332	THEOREM FS_Difference ==
333	  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S)
334	  PROVE /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ T)
335	        /\ Cardinality(S \ T) = Cardinality(S) - Cardinality(S \cap T)
336	
337	
338	(***************************************************************************)
339	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
340	(*                                                                         *)
341	(* The union of a finite number of finite sets is finite.                  *)
342	(*                                                                         *)
343	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
344	(***************************************************************************)
345	THEOREM FS_UNION ==
346	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), \A T \in S : IsFiniteSet(T)
347	  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(UNION S)
348	
349	
350	(***************************************************************************)
351	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
352	(*                                                                         *)
353	(* The product of two finite sets is finite.                               *)
354	(*                                                                         *)
355	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
356	(***************************************************************************)
357	THEOREM FS_Product ==
358	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
359	         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T)
360	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \X T)
361	         /\ Cardinality(S \X T) = Cardinality(S) * Cardinality(T)
362	
363	
364	(***************************************************************************)
365	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
366	(*                                                                         *)
367	(* The powerset of a finite set is finite.                                 *)
368	(*                                                                         *)
369	(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
370	(***************************************************************************)
371	THEOREM FS_SUBSET ==
372	  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
373	  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(SUBSET S)
374	         /\ Cardinality(SUBSET S) = 2^Cardinality(S)
375	
376	=============================================================================

------------------------- MODULE FiniteSetTheorems --------------------------
(***************************************************************************)
(* `^{\large\bf \vspace{12pt}                                              *)
(*  Facts about finite sets and their cardinality.                         *)
(*  Originally contributed by Tom Rodeheffer, MSR.                         *)
(*  \vspace{12pt}}^'                                                       *)
(***************************************************************************)

EXTENDS FiniteSets, Integers, Functions, WellFoundedInduction

(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* A set S is finite iff there exists a natural number n such that there   *)
(* exist a surjection (or a bijection) from 1..n to S.                     *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)

LEMMA FS_NatSurjection ==
  ASSUME NEW S
  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(S) <=> \E n \in Nat : ExistsSurjection(1..n,S)

LEMMA FS_NatBijection ==
  ASSUME NEW S
  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(S) <=> \E n \in Nat : ExistsBijection(1..n,S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* If there exists n \in Nat such that a bijection exists from 1..n to S,  *)
(* then Cardinality(S) = n.                                                *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)

LEMMA FS_CountingElements ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsBijection(1..n,S)
  PROVE  Cardinality(S) = n


(***************************************************************************)
(* Corollary: a surjection from 1..n to S provides a cardinality bound.    *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_SurjCardinalityBound ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsSurjection(1..n, S)
  PROVE  Cardinality(S) <= n


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* For any finite set S, Cardinality(S) \in Nat. Moreover, there is a      *)
(* bijection from 1 .. Cardinality(S) to S.                                *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_CardinalityType ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ Cardinality(S) \in Nat
         /\ ExistsBijection(1..Cardinality(S), S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The image of a finite set under a bijection is finite.                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Bijection ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), ExistsBijection(S,T)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
         /\ Cardinality(T) = Cardinality(S)

THEOREM FS_SameCardinalityBij ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), IsFiniteSet(T),
         Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T)
  PROVE  ExistsBijection(S,T)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Any surjection between two finite sets of equal cardinality is          *)
(* an injection.                                                           *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_SurjSameCardinalityImpliesInj ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S), Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T),
         NEW f \in Surjection(S,T)
  PROVE  f \in Injection(S,T)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The image of a finite set is finite.                                    *)
(* NB: Note that any function is a surjection on its range by theorem      *)
(*     Fun_RangeProperties.                                                *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Surjection ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Surjection(S,T), IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
         /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)
         /\ Cardinality(T) = Cardinality(S) <=> f \in Injection(S,T)

THEOREM FS_Image ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW Op(_)
  PROVE  LET T == {Op(x) : x \in S}
         IN  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
             /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)

(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Symmetric theorem for injections and the pigeonhole principle.          *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Injection ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, NEW f \in Injection(S,T), IsFiniteSet(T)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
         /\ Cardinality(S) <= Cardinality(T)
         /\ Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T) <=> f \in Surjection(S,T)

THEOREM FS_PigeonHole ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T), Cardinality(T) < Cardinality(S),
         NEW f \in [S -> T]
  PROVE  \E x,y \in S : x # y /\ f[x] = f[y]


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The cardinality of a finite set S is 0 iff S is empty.                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_EmptySet ==
  /\ IsFiniteSet({})
  /\ Cardinality({}) = 0
  /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => (Cardinality(S) = 0 <=> S = {})


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* If S is finite, so are S \cup {x} and S \ {x}.                          *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_AddElement ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW x, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup {x})
         /\ Cardinality(S \cup {x}) =
            IF x \in S THEN Cardinality(S) ELSE Cardinality(S)+1

THEOREM FS_RemoveElement ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW x, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ {x})
         /\ Cardinality(S \ {x}) =
            IF x \in S THEN Cardinality(S)-1 ELSE Cardinality(S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* In particular, a singleton set is finite.                               *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Singleton ==
  /\ \A x : IsFiniteSet({x}) /\ Cardinality({x}) = 1
  /\ \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) => (Cardinality(S) = 1 <=> \E x: S = {x})


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Any subset of a finite set is finite.                                   *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Subset ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T \in SUBSET S
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(T)
         /\ Cardinality(T) <= Cardinality(S)
         /\ Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(T) => S = T


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* a..b is a finite set for any a,b \in Int.                               *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Interval ==
  ASSUME NEW a \in Int, NEW b \in Int
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(a..b)
         /\ Cardinality(a..b) = IF a > b THEN 0 ELSE b-a+1

THEOREM FS_BoundedSetOfNaturals ==
  ASSUME NEW S \in SUBSET Nat, NEW n \in Nat,
         \A s \in S : s <= n
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
         /\ Cardinality(S) \leq n+1


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Induction for finite sets.                                              *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)

THEOREM FS_Induction ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         NEW P(_), P({}),
         ASSUME NEW T \in SUBSET S, IsFiniteSet(T), P(T), NEW x \in S \ T
         PROVE  P(T \cup {x})
  PROVE  P(S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The finite subsets form a well-founded ordering with respect to strict  *)
(* set inclusion.                                                          *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)

FiniteSubsetsOf(S) == { T \in SUBSET S : IsFiniteSet(T) }
StrictSubsetOrdering(S) == { ss \in (SUBSET S) \X (SUBSET S) :
                                ss[1] \subseteq ss[2] /\ ss[1] # ss[2] }

LEMMA FS_FiniteSubsetsOfFinite ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  FiniteSubsetsOf(S) = SUBSET S


(*****************************************************************************)
(*  The formulation of the following theorem doesn't require S being finite. *)
(*  If S is finite, it implies                                               *)
(*       IsWellFoundedOn(StrictSubsetOrdering(S), SUBSET S)                  *)
(*  using lemma FS_FiniteSubsetsOfFinite.                                    *)
(*****************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_StrictSubsetOrderingWellFounded ==
  ASSUME NEW S
  PROVE  IsWellFoundedOn(StrictSubsetOrdering(S), FiniteSubsetsOf(S))


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Well-founded induction for finite subsets.                              *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)

THEOREM FS_WFInduction ==
  ASSUME NEW P(_), NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         ASSUME NEW T \in SUBSET S,
                \A U \in (SUBSET T) \ {T} : P(U)
         PROVE  P(T)
  PROVE  P(S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The union of two finite sets is finite.                                 *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Union ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup T)
         /\ Cardinality(S \cup T) =
               Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T) - Cardinality(S \cap T)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* Corollary: two majorities intersect. More precisely, any two subsets    *)
(* of a finite set U such that the sum of cardinalities of the subsets     *)
(* exceeds that of U must have non-empty intersection.                     *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_MajoritiesIntersect ==
  ASSUME NEW U, NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(U),
         S \subseteq U, T \subseteq U,
         Cardinality(S) + Cardinality(T) > Cardinality(U)
  PROVE  S \cap T # {}


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The intersection of a finite set with an arbitrary set is finite.       *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)


THEOREM FS_Intersection ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), NEW T
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cap T)
         /\ IsFiniteSet(T \cap S)
         /\ Cardinality(S \cap T) <= Cardinality(S)
         /\ Cardinality(T \cap S) <= Cardinality(S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The difference between a finite set and an arbitrary set is finite.     *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Difference ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW T, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE /\ IsFiniteSet(S \ T)
        /\ Cardinality(S \ T) = Cardinality(S) - Cardinality(S \cap T)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The union of a finite number of finite sets is finite.                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_UNION ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S), \A T \in S : IsFiniteSet(T)
  PROVE  IsFiniteSet(UNION S)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The product of two finite sets is finite.                               *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_Product ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \X T)
         /\ Cardinality(S \X T) = Cardinality(S) * Cardinality(T)


(***************************************************************************)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* The powerset of a finite set is finite.                                 *)
(*                                                                         *)
(* `.  .'                                                                  *)
(***************************************************************************)
THEOREM FS_SUBSET ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(SUBSET S)
         /\ Cardinality(SUBSET S) = 2^Cardinality(S)

content.text: Now let me write the proof. I'll do induction on the cardinality of TT.

input.content:
-------------------------------- MODULE Sets_FiniteSubset --------------------------------
EXTENDS Integers, NaturalsInduction, TLAPS

IsBijection(f, S, T) == /\ f \in [S -> T]
                        /\ \A x, y \in S : (x # y) => (f[x] # f[y])
                        /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : f[x] = y

IsFiniteSet(S) == \E n \in Nat : \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)

CONSTANT Cardinality(_)
AXIOM CardinalityAxiom ==
         \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) =>
           \A n : (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
                    (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

ExistsBij(n, S) == \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)

------------------------------------------------------------------

LEMMA EmptyInterval == 1..0 = {}
PROOF OBVIOUS

LEMMA EmptyBijection == IsBijection([x \in {} |-> x], 1..0, {})
PROOF
  <1> DEFINE f == [x \in {} |-> x]
  <1>1. 1..0 = {} BY EmptyInterval
  <1>2. f \in [{} -> {}] OBVIOUS
  <1>3. f \in [1..0 -> {}] BY <1>1, <1>2
  <1>4. \A x, y \in 1..0 : (x # y) => (f[x] # f[y])
    BY <1>1
  <1>5. \A y \in {} : \E x \in 1..0 : f[x] = y
    OBVIOUS
  <1>6. QED BY <1>3, <1>4, <1>5 DEF IsBijection

LEMMA EmptyFinite == IsFiniteSet({})
PROOF
  <1>1. \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..0, {}) BY EmptyBijection
  <1>2. 0 \in Nat OBVIOUS
  <1>3. \E n \in Nat : \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, {}) BY <1>1, <1>2
  <1>4. QED BY <1>3 DEF IsFiniteSet

LEMMA CardCharacterization ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S)
  PROVE  /\ Cardinality(S) \in Nat
         /\ ExistsBij(Cardinality(S), S)
PROOF
  <1>1. \A n : (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
              (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
    BY CardinalityAxiom
  <1>2. (Cardinality(S) = Cardinality(S)) <=>
        (Cardinality(S) \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..Cardinality(S), S)
    BY <1>1
  <1>3. (Cardinality(S) \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..Cardinality(S), S)
    BY <1>2
  <1>4. QED BY <1>3 DEF ExistsBij

LEMMA BijImpliesCard ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW n \in Nat, ExistsBij(n, S)
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S)
         /\ Cardinality(S) = n
PROOF
  <1>1. IsFiniteSet(S) BY DEF IsFiniteSet, ExistsBij
  <1>2. \A m : (m = Cardinality(S)) <=>
              (m \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..m, S)
    BY <1>1, CardinalityAxiom
  <1>3. (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
        (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
    BY <1>2
  <1>4. (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)
    BY DEF ExistsBij
  <1>5. n = Cardinality(S) BY <1>3, <1>4
  <1>6. QED BY <1>1, <1>5

LEMMA EmptyCard == Cardinality({}) = 0
PROOF
  <1>1. IsFiniteSet({}) BY EmptyFinite
  <1>2. ExistsBij(0, {}) BY EmptyBijection DEF ExistsBij
  <1>3. 0 \in Nat OBVIOUS
  <1>4. QED BY <1>2, <1>3, BijImpliesCard

LEMMA ZeroCardEmpty ==
  ASSUME NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T), Cardinality(T) = 0
  PROVE  T = {}
PROOF
  <1>1. ExistsBij(Cardinality(T), T) BY CardCharacterization
  <1>2. ExistsBij(0, T) BY <1>1
  <1>3. PICK f : IsBijection(f, 1..0, T) BY <1>2 DEF ExistsBij
  <1>4. \A y \in T : \E xx \in 1..0 : f[xx] = y BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>5. 1..0 = {} BY EmptyInterval
  <1>6. QED BY <1>4, <1>5

------------------------------------------------------------------

LEMMA RemoveElementCard ==
  ASSUME NEW T, IsFiniteSet(T),
         NEW n \in Nat, Cardinality(T) = n+1
  PROVE  \E x \in T :
            /\ IsFiniteSet(T \ {x})
            /\ Cardinality(T \ {x}) = n
PROOF
  <1>1. ExistsBij(Cardinality(T), T) BY CardCharacterization
  <1>2. ExistsBij(n+1, T) BY <1>1
  <1>3. PICK f : IsBijection(f, 1..(n+1), T) BY <1>2 DEF ExistsBij
  <1>4. f \in [1..(n+1) -> T] BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>5. \A i, j \in 1..(n+1) : (i # j) => (f[i] # f[j]) BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>6. \A y \in T : \E i \in 1..(n+1) : f[i] = y BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>7. n+1 \in 1..(n+1) OBVIOUS
  <1> DEFINE x == f[n+1]
  <1>8. x \in T BY <1>4, <1>7
  <1> DEFINE g == [i \in 1..n |-> f[i]]
  <1>9. \A i \in 1..n : g[i] = f[i] OBVIOUS
  <1>10. \A i \in 1..n : i \in 1..(n+1) OBVIOUS
  <1>11. \A i \in 1..n : i # n+1 OBVIOUS
  <1>12. \A i \in 1..n : f[i] # x
    <2> TAKE i \in 1..n
    <2>1. i \in 1..(n+1) BY <1>10
    <2>2. i # n+1 BY <1>11
    <2>3. f[i] # f[n+1] BY <2>1, <2>2, <1>5, <1>7
    <2>4. QED BY <2>3
  <1>13. \A i \in 1..n : g[i] \in T \ {x}
    <2> TAKE i \in 1..n
    <2>1. g[i] = f[i] BY <1>9
    <2>2. f[i] \in T BY <1>10, <1>4
    <2>3. f[i] # x BY <1>12
    <2>4. QED BY <2>1, <2>2, <2>3
  <1>14. g \in [1..n -> T \ {x}]
    BY <1>13
  <1>15. \A i, j \in 1..n : (i # j) => (g[i] # g[j])
    <2> TAKE i, j \in 1..n
    <2> HAVE i # j
    <2>1. i \in 1..(n+1) /\ j \in 1..(n+1) BY <1>10
    <2>2. f[i] # f[j] BY <2>1, <1>5
    <2>3. g[i] = f[i] /\ g[j] = f[j] BY <1>9
    <2>4. QED BY <2>2, <2>3
  <1>16. \A y \in T \ {x} : \E i \in 1..n : g[i] = y
    <2> TAKE y \in T \ {x}
    <2>1. y \in T OBVIOUS
    <2>2. PICK i \in 1..(n+1) : f[i] = y BY <2>1, <1>6
    <2>3. y # x OBVIOUS
    <2>4. f[n+1] = x OBVIOUS
    <2>5. i # n+1 BY <2>2, <2>3, <2>4
    <2>6. i \in 1..n BY <2>2, <2>5
    <2>7. g[i] = f[i] BY <2>6, <1>9
    <2>8. g[i] = y BY <2>7, <2>2
    <2>9. QED BY <2>6, <2>8
  <1>17. IsBijection(g, 1..n, T \ {x}) BY <1>14, <1>15, <1>16 DEF IsBijection
  <1>18. ExistsBij(n, T \ {x}) BY <1>17 DEF ExistsBij
  <1>19. IsFiniteSet(T \ {x}) /\ Cardinality(T \ {x}) = n BY <1>18, BijImpliesCard
  <1>20. QED BY <1>8, <1>19

LEMMA AddElementCard ==
  ASSUME NEW S, IsFiniteSet(S),
         NEW x, x \notin S,
         NEW m \in Nat, Cardinality(S) = m
  PROVE  /\ IsFiniteSet(S \cup {x})
         /\ Cardinality(S \cup {x}) = m+1
PROOF
  <1>1. ExistsBij(Cardinality(S), S) BY CardCharacterization
  <1>2. ExistsBij(m, S) BY <1>1
  <1>3. PICK f : IsBijection(f, 1..m, S) BY <1>2 DEF ExistsBij
  <1>4. f \in [1..m -> S] BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>5. \A i, j \in 1..m : (i # j) => (f[i] # f[j]) BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1>6. \A y \in S : \E i \in 1..m : f[i] = y BY <1>3 DEF IsBijection
  <1> DEFINE g == [i \in 1..(m+1) |-> IF i = m+1 THEN x ELSE f[i]]
  <1>7. m+1 \in 1..(m+1) OBVIOUS
  <1>8. g[m+1] = x OBVIOUS
  <1>9. \A i \in 1..m : g[i] = f[i]
    <2> TAKE i \in 1..m
    <2>1. i \in 1..(m+1) OBVIOUS
    <2>2. i # m+1 OBVIOUS
    <2>3. QED BY <2>1, <2>2
  <1>10. g \in [1..(m+1) -> S \cup {x}]
    <2> TAKE i \in 1..(m+1)
    <2>1. CASE i = m+1
      <3>1. g[i] = x BY <2>1
      <3>2. x \in S \cup {x} OBVIOUS
      <3>3. QED BY <3>1, <3>2
    <2>2. CASE i # m+1
      <3>1. i \in 1..m BY <2>2
      <3>2. g[i] = f[i] BY <3>1, <1>9
      <3>3. f[i] \in S BY <3>1, <1>4
      <3>4. f[i] \in S \cup {x} BY <3>3
      <3>5. QED BY <3>2, <3>4
    <2>3. QED BY <2>1, <2>2
  <1>11. \A i, j \in 1..(m+1) : (i # j) => (g[i] # g[j])
    <2> TAKE i, j \in 1..(m+1)
    <2> HAVE i # j
    <2>1. CASE i = m+1 /\ j # m+1
      <3>1. g[i] = x BY <2>1
      <3>2. j \in 1..m BY <2>1
      <3>3. g[j] = f[j] BY <3>2, <1>9
      <3>4. f[j] \in S BY <3>2, <1>4
      <3>5. g[j] \in S BY <3>3, <3>4
      <3>6. g[j] # x BY <3>5
      <3>7. QED BY <3>1, <3>6
    <2>2. CASE j = m+1 /\ i # m+1
      <3>1. g[j] = x BY <2>2
      <3>2. i \in 1..m BY <2>2
      <3>3. g[i] = f[i] BY <3>2, <1>9
      <3>4. f[i] \in S BY <3>2, <1>4
      <3>5. g[i] \in S BY <3>3, <3>4
      <3>6. g[i] # x BY <3>5
      <3>7. QED BY <3>1, <3>6
    <2>3. CASE i # m+1 /\ j # m+1
      <3>1. i \in 1..m /\ j \in 1..m BY <2>3
      <3>2. g[i] = f[i] /\ g[j] = f[j] BY <3>1, <1>9
      <3>3. f[i] # f[j] BY <3>1, <1>5
      <3>4. QED BY <3>2, <3>3
    <2>4. QED BY <2>1, <2>2, <2>3
  <1>12. \A y \in S \cup {x} : \E i \in 1..(m+1) : g[i] = y
    <2> TAKE y \in S \cup {x}
    <2>1. CASE y = x
      <3>1. g[m+1] = x BY <1>8
      <3>2. g[m+1] = y BY <3>1, <2>1
      <3>3. QED BY <1>7, <3>2
    <2>2. CASE y # x
      <3>1. y \in S BY <2>2
      <3>2. PICK i \in 1..m : f[i] = y BY <3>1, <1>6
      <3>3. g[i] = f[i] BY <3>2, <1>9
      <3>4. g[i] = y BY <3>3, <3>2
      <3>5. i \in 1..(m+1) BY <3>2
      <3>6. QED BY <3>4, <3>5
    <2>3. QED BY <2>1, <2>2
  <1>13. IsBijection(g, 1..(m+1), S \cup {x})
    BY <1>10, <1>11, <1>12 DEF IsBijection
  <1>14. ExistsBij(m+1, S \cup {x}) BY <1>13 DEF ExistsBij
  <1>15. m+1 \in Nat OBVIOUS
  <1>16. QED BY <1>14, <1>15, BijImpliesCard

------------------------------------------------------------------

THEOREM FiniteSubset ==
  ASSUME NEW S, NEW TT, IsFiniteSet(TT), S \subseteq TT

IsBijection(f, S, T) == /\ f \in [S -> T]
                        /\ \A x, y \in S : (x # y) => (f[x] # f[y])
                        /\ \A y \in T : \E x \in S : f[x] = y

IsFiniteSet(S) == \E n \in Nat : \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)

CONSTANT Cardinality(_)
AXIOM CardinalityAxiom ==
         \A S : IsFiniteSet(S) =>
           \A n : (n = Cardinality(S)) <=>
                    (n \in Nat) /\ \E f : IsBijection(f, 1..n, S)

  <1>6. QED BY <1>3, <1>4, <1>5 DEF IsBijection
